glossaryTerms.definition: a line-inlay technique in which incised lines are filled with slip then the surface is scraped back to reveal the inlay
the drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim, carve, and join
fully air-dried but unfired clay: fragile and at maximum absorbency
clay that has been through the first firing (cone 06, approximately 1000 degrees C) and is now hard but unglazed and porous
a pyrometric measure of heat-work: cone 06 is approximately 1000 degrees C, cone 6 is approximately 1220 degrees C

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no compressed air, no dry sanding indoors.
Ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln. Never open a hot kiln below approximately 80 degrees C.

Mishima is a Korean and Japanese inlay tradition. Lines are incised into leather-hard clay, packed with contrasting slip, and allowed to stiffen. The surface is then scraped back so only the slip in the incised lines stays visible. On thrown stoneware, the process continues through bisque and glaze firings. The bisque firing bonds the inlaid slip to the clay body. A transparent or semi-transparent glaze fired to cone 6 (about 1220 degrees C) protects the design. The result is a fine white line design set flush in the stoneware surface, watertight and permanent. Unlike mishima on air-dry clay, this version is kiln-fired, making it food-safe and durable.
is a Korean and Japanese inlay tradition: lines are incised into
leather-hard

clay, packed with contrasting slip, allowed to stiffen, then scraped back so that only the slip trapped in the incised lines remains visible. On thrown stoneware the process continues through bisque and glaze firings: the
bisque
firing bonds the inlaid slip permanently to the clay body, and a transparent or semi-transparent glaze fired to
cone

6 (approximately 1220 degrees C) preserves and protects the design. The result is a fine white line design embedded flush in the stoneware surface, watertight and permanent. This version is distinct from
mishima
on air-dry clay in that the piece is kiln-fired, making it food-safe and durable.
When to use it

Apply mishima to any thrown stoneware piece at leather-hard. The technique suits cylindrical forms where the line work is easily visible: mugs, vases, and tall cylindrical bowls are all good candidates. It reads best on a smooth stoneware body where the contrast between the white slip and the natural clay colour is strong. The design is incised before trimming the foot ring, so the entire surface decoration can be planned as part of a single leather-hard session. Mishima is not compatible with heavily textured surfaces or with clay bodies containing coarse grog, because the scraping stage tears the texture rather than removing it cleanly.
What you need

materials.name: White coloured slip
materials.quantity: small pot, enough to fill incised lines generously

content.text: Step by step

Confirm the piece is at leather-hard: it should feel cool and firm, hold its shape without wobble, and show no shiny wet surface. Place it on a banding wheel so you can rotate it without picking it up.

Sketch or trace your design lightly onto the surface using a soft pencil or by impressing a paper template through the wall. Lines need to be at least 1 mm wide to hold slip reliably. Very fine lines close together produce a delicate result; widely spaced bold lines produce a graphic result.

Incise the lines using the needle tool or sgraffito tool. Cut with a smooth, even pressure, keeping the line depth consistent at approximately 1 to 2 mm. Deeper cuts hold more slip and produce a bolder line; shallower cuts produce a finer line but are harder to fill without leaving excess on the surface. Avoid dragging the tool sideways at the end of a stroke, which tears the clay.

Load the white slip into the incised lines using the liner brush. Work generously: fill each line until the slip is proud of the surface by 1 to 2 mm. Ensure every part of each line is filled, including corners and line junctions, which tend to receive less slip than straight sections.

Allow the slip to stiffen in the lines until it has lost its sheen and matches the stiffness of the surrounding leather-hard clay. This typically takes 20 to 40 minutes. Do not proceed to scraping while the slip is still wet: the scraping tool will drag wet slip out of the lines.

Scrape back the surface using the flat wooden rib held at a low angle, approximately 30 degrees from the clay surface. Draw it firmly across the clay, removing the excess slip from the surface while leaving the slip embedded in the incised lines. Work in single passes in the same direction to avoid smearing. The surface should reveal clean lines of white against the natural clay colour after two or three passes.
Allow the piece to dry to
bone-dry

before bisque firing. The inlaid slip shrinks at the same rate as the surrounding clay body; if the clay bodies are compatible, no cracking occurs at the inlay lines during drying. Use the same stoneware body for the piece and check that the white slip is formulated for compatibility with that body.

Bisque fire to cone 06 (approximately 1000 degrees C). Glaze with a transparent or semi-transparent mid-fire glaze: a fully opaque glaze will cover the mishima lines and negate the surface decoration. A clear glaze preserves the white-on-stoneware contrast cleanly. Glaze fire to cone 6 (approximately 1220 degrees C).
What it looks like when it works

A successful mishima inlay shows white lines sitting flush with the clay surface, with clean edges and no feathering at the line boundaries. The surrounding clay body is clean: no white residue from imperfect scraping, no dragged smears between lines. Under a clear glaze, the lines appear slightly more luminous than unglazed white slip because the glaze refracts light back through itself. The design is permanent: it cannot be scratched off or washed away.
Common mistakes

Scraping too soon: the slip is still semi-liquid and comes out of the incised line entirely rather than staying in place. Wait until the slip and the surrounding clay are at the same stiffness before scraping.
Scraping too late (clay past leather-hard): the rib tears the dry clay surface, leaving scratch marks across the decoration. The scraping stage requires leather-hard clay, not bone-dry clay.
Slip too thin: a very dilute slip flows out of the incised lines rather than filling them. The slip should have a thick cream consistency that stays where placed without running.
Using an opaque glaze over the mishima lines: the lines disappear under the glaze. Check the glaze is transparent or at most semi-transparent before committing to the glaze firing.

Lines cracking at the inlay boundary after drying: the slip and the clay body have incompatible shrinkage rates. Use a slip formulated specifically for the clay body you are working with, or add a small amount of the native clay to the slip to match shrinkage.
